Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • 01Identify the purpose of a map.
  • 02Distinguish between a map and a geovisualization.
  • 03Identify key map elements.
  • 04Think critically about maps.

The GIS workflow ends with a map

Most GIS work — collecting data, modeling, analyzing — builds toward a single deliverable: a map that communicates what you found.

What is a map?

Definitions vary, but they share a core idea — a map is a symbolized, reduced-scale representation of geographic reality:

  • 1A symbolized image of geographic reality — selected features, shaped by the mapmaker’s choices, for when spatial relationships matter. (Clark, 2011)
  • 2A graphic depiction of a place where real features are replaced by symbols in their correct positions at a reduced scale. (ICA, 1995)
  • 3A digital or analog medium for storing and communicating geographic information. (Longley et al., 2015)

What are maps for?

A map is a medium for communicating information:

  • ›Deliver a message or tell a story
  • ›Problem solving
  • ›Decision support

And an enhanced visual for understanding the world — spatial patterns, spatial relationships, and comparisons over time.

Maps tell a story

Every map makes choices about what to show and how — so every map argues something.

Types of maps

Different jobs call for different kinds of maps:

TypeWhat it shows
Road mapHelps with navigation.
Physical mapLand and water features as they occur in nature.
Pictorial mapUses drawings to indicate features — buildings, forests, landmarks.
Political mapCountries, capitals, states, provinces.
Relief mapCaptures the 3-D aspects of the Earth.
Topographic mapShows elevation in a 2-D representation.
Geological mapShows the underlying geology.
Thematic mapFocuses on a specific theme.

Thematic maps

Thematic maps zero in on one theme — and they’re the workhorse of GIS communication.

Hardcopy → digital maps

Hardcopy maps are drawn, written, or printed on physical media. In the early years of GIS, most data were converted from hardcopy via digitizing — the process of collecting digital coordinates from maps.

Digital maps are an electronic, graphic depiction of spatial data — now our most common map form. The interactive kind are often called geovisualizations.

Hardcopy mapDigital map
Fixed scaleZoom allows viewing at a range of scales
Static and unchangingSeamless coverage, often dynamic
Presents a static view of the worldAnimations and interactions
FlatCan provide 3-D visualization
Map-producer-centric viewUser-centric view
Map vs. geovisualization: a static, producer-authored map presents a finished message; an interactive geovisualization hands the reader tools to explore the data themselves.

Accuracy, resolution & scale

Accuracy is how close a value is to the truth. Spatial resolution is the smallest feature that can be individually resolved in a dataset or display.

Scale

All displayed spatial data have a scale: the relationship between a distance on the screen or paper and the corresponding distance on Earth. It’s recorded as a distance conversion — e.g. one inch = one mile, a ratio like 1:24,000 (one unit on the display = 24,000 units on Earth), or a scale bar.

Group brainstorm
Which is large scale and which is small scale — 1:24,000 or 1:100,000?
Answer 1:24,000 is the large-scale map. A larger ratio (1/24,000 > 1/100,000) means a large-scale map — features appear larger and you see more local detail over a smaller area.

Data generalization

To generalize a map means to simplify or abstract the data so it can be better understood. As scale shrinks, generalization becomes unavoidable. A cartographer has a toolkit of moves:

OperatorWhat it does
ExaggerationMake important features bigger/bolder than “true size.”
DisplacementShift features slightly to avoid overlap.
TypificationReduce the number of features but keep the pattern.
ReclassificationRe-group detailed categories into fewer classes.
AggregationCombine smaller units/features into bigger ones.
SimplificationReduce the vertices/complexity of lines and polygons.
RefinementClean and adjust shapes for clarity and aesthetics.

Cartography, symbology & labels

Cartography is the art, science, and techniques of making maps. The same data becomes a map only once we add symbols, a legend, and the other elements that make it readable.

Symbology

Symbology uses visual variables — size, color, shape, value — to represent data. Different thematic techniques suit different data:

Classification — and lying with maps

Quantitative (interval and ratio) data are usually grouped into classes before mapping — then each class gets a color (a choropleth map) or a symbol size (a graduated symbol map). How you draw those class breaks is a design choice, and it changes the story the map tells.

Four common classification methods

Here is the same dataset — interpolated arsenic concentrations near Langley, BC — classified four different ways. Same numbers, four very different maps:

Same data, four maps. No single classification is “correct” — each highlights something different. That power is exactly why a map can mislead.

Choropleth colors

Use a change in saturation or hue to signal larger quantities. Avoid rainbow schemes for quantitative maps — too many colors make a map harder to read and hide the order in the data.

Lying with maps

Because every classification, color, and boundary is a choice, making bad maps is easy with GIS — and maps can mislead, accidentally or on purpose. Mark Monmonier’s classic How to Lie with Maps is all about reading those choices critically. Keep that lens handy for the map audit coming up.

Map elements

A finished map layout is built from a recognizable set of parts:

  • ›Data pane — the map itself
  • ›Neatlines — framing borders
  • ›Scale bar
  • ›Legend
  • ›North arrow
  • ›Title
  • ›Additional information (source, author, date)

Key cartographic principles

Before placing anything, ask: What is the purpose? Who is the audience? What is the medium? Under what conditions will it be viewed?

Visual hierarchy

Visual hierarchy is the order in which a reader perceives the elements of a map — shaped by the cartographer’s choices of balance, arrangement, negative space, and symbols.

A handful of design moves shape that hierarchy:

Bad maps

Every choice can mislead. These maps stumble on classification, color, balance, or honesty — can you say why each one is hard to read or read it the wrong way?

Activity · discussion board

“Spot the Lie” map audit

Find two maps online about the same topic (election results, crime, COVID, housing prices, …). For each map, write a short audit based on the Monmonier reading:

  • ?What’s the argument the map is making?
  • ?What choices shape that argument? (classification, color scheme, projection, symbol sizes, boundaries, aggregation)
  • ?What’s not shown that matters?
  • ?Who is the audience, and what action might the map encourage?

Then decide: which map is more honest, and why? Propose one redesign to reduce bias or increase clarity. Post both maps (with links) and your discussion to the course discussion board.

Key takeaways

1
Maps are the standard output of a GIS workflow.
2
A map is a symbolized representation of reality — not reality itself.
3
Maps tell stories, because every map involves choices.
4
Digital mapping changed what maps can do — zoom, interaction, animation, 3-D.
5
Scale drives what detail is appropriate.
6
Generalization is unavoidable.
7
Layouts should be intentional — core elements (scale bar, legend, title) plus sound design principles.

Key terms & concepts

Open any term to see its definition.
